title: Compare Quantities by Position
description: Determine which quantity is greater or lesser by comparing their positions on a number line.
condition: Two or more labeled points are shown on the same number line.
response: Identify the point farthest to the right as greatest and farthest to the left as least.
label: Principle / Model
example: If Elena is at 15, Andre at 28, and Clare at 38, then 38 is greatest and 15 is least.

title: Identify Midpoint of an Interval
description: Determine the number halfway between two labeled endpoints on a number line.
condition: Two labeled endpoints on a number line (e.g., 0 and 80).
response: Compute or state the midpoint value between the endpoints.
label: Principle / Model

title: Interpret Jump Direction
description: Determine whether a jump on a number line represents addition or subtraction based on its direction.
condition: A visual jump on a number line pointing either left or right.
response: Identify rightward jumps as addition and leftward jumps as subtraction.
label: Concept
example: A jump from 34 to 43 moving right represents addition.

title: Locate and Label a Number
description: Place a given number accurately on a number line using equal spacing and benchmarks.
condition: A number line with labeled intervals is shown and a specific number is given.
response: Mark the position corresponding to that number at the correct tick mark.
label: Production / Skill
example: On a 0–50 number line with unit ticks, place 43 three units to the right of 40.

title: Locate Number Relative to Nearest Benchmark
description: Place or identify a number on a number line by comparing it to the nearest labeled multiple (e.g., nearest ten).
condition: A target whole number within 100 and nearby labeled benchmark numbers (e.g., 30 and 40).
response: Determine whether the number is to the left or right of the benchmark and place it the correct number of intervals away.
label: Principle / Model
example: To locate 34, note it is between 30 and 40 and 4 units to the right of 30.

title: Match Expression to Number Line Representation
description: Match a numerical expression to a number line by aligning starting point, direction, and jump size.
condition: A number line diagram showing a starting point, direction of movement, and distance moved.
response: Select or write the expression whose first number is the starting point and whose operation and second number match the direction and size of the jump.
label: Principle / Model
example: A jump starting at 34 and moving 9 units right matches $34 + 9$.

title: Recognize Equal Spacing on Number Line
description: Understand that equal distances between tick marks represent equal numerical increments.
condition: A number line with uniformly spaced tick marks.
response: Infer that each interval represents the same numerical increase.
label: Concept
example: If the فاصلة between 0 and 10 is divided into 10 equal أجزاء, each interval represents 1.

title: Represent Addition as Rightward Jumps
description: Represent addition on a number line by starting at the first addend and making rightward jumps equal to the second addend.
condition: An addition expression a + b is to be represented on a number line.
response: Start at a and move right a total distance of b (possibly in parts) to locate the sum.
label: Principle / Model
example: To represent $25 + 44$, start at 25 and move right 44 units to land on 69.

title: Represent Subtraction as Leftward Jumps
description: Represent subtraction on a number line by starting at the minuend and making leftward jumps equal to the subtrahend.
condition: A subtraction expression a − b is to be represented on a number line.
response: Start at a and move left a total distance of b to locate the difference.
label: Principle / Model
example: To represent $53 - 17$, start at 53 and move left 17 units to land on 36.

title: Represent Word Problem with Equation
description: Translate a word problem describing a total or increase into an addition equation with an unknown.
condition: A word problem stating a starting amount and an additional amount, asking for the total.
response: Write an equation of the form start + increase = ?.
label: Principle / Model
example: Bracelet is 17 cm and necklace is 38 cm longer: $17 + 38 = ?$.

title: Use Relative Position to Estimate Value
description: Estimate the value of a point by judging its position relative to known reference points (e.g., midpoint).
condition: A point placed on a number line between two labeled endpoints without intermediate labels.
response: Estimate a reasonable numerical value based on proximity to known reference points.
label: Principle / Model
example: If a point is slightly left of the midpoint 40 on a 0–80 number line, a reasonable estimate is about $30$.
